whyCorrect: For a tube closed at one end, successive resonance lengths differ by half a wavelength, so resonant frequencies are odd harmonics of the fundamental. The next resonance after the lowest corresponds to the third harmonic, giving $f_2/f_1 = 3$.

whyOthersWrong.A: 8 would not match the usual harmonic progression for this setup.
whyOthersWrong.C: 2 would correspond to a full-octave ratio typical of open–open pipes, not this closed-tube pattern.
whyOthersWrong.D: ½ would imply the second frequency is lower than the first, contrary to “increasing frequency”.
whyOthersWrong.E: 1/3 is the reciprocal of the correct ratio.

whyCorrect: Altitude (height above a reference) is a scalar: it has magnitude only. Force, weight, displacement, and acceleration are vectors.

whyOthersWrong.A: Force has magnitude and direction.
whyOthersWrong.C: Weight is the gravitational force; it is a vector.
whyOthersWrong.D: Displacement is a vector.

whyCorrect: For three forces in equilibrium, resolving perpendicular components gives vertical balance including $P_3$ balanced by vertical components of $P_1$ and $P_2$ (statement II in standard forms), and horizontal balance $P_1\sin\theta_1 = P_2\sin\theta_2$ (statement III). Statement I is generally not written that way for arbitrary angles.

whyOthersWrong.A: I alone is not generally the correct pair of equations.
whyOthersWrong.B: II alone ignores the usual horizontal equilibrium relation.
whyOthersWrong.C: III alone ignores vertical equilibrium involving $P_3$.
whyOthersWrong.E: I and III together wrongly treats I as correct.

whyCorrect: The angle of friction (angle of repose) is defined between the **resultant** contact force and the normal reaction, not between the normal and the friction force alone.

whyOthersWrong.A: Kinetic friction is typically less than limiting static friction — correct statement.
whyOthersWrong.B: Kinetic friction independent of contact area (given constant normal reaction) is correct for the usual model.
whyOthersWrong.C: Rolling friction is generally less than sliding friction — correct.

whyCorrect: When the brick remains at rest, static friction exactly balances the applied horizontal pull; if the pull increases without motion, static friction increases up to its limiting value.

whyOthersWrong.B: Friction on the brick from the table acts opposite to impending motion — not “to the right” as a blanket statement.
whyOthersWrong.C: Adding mass increases normal reaction and usually **increases** maximum static friction, not decreases it.
whyOthersWrong.D: While sliding, kinetic friction is not necessarily zero.
whyOthersWrong.E: Turning the brick can change effective contact and friction in some models, but the key idea tested here is static equilibrium with increasing pull.

whyCorrect: The gravitational pull of the Earth on a body is its weight $W=mg$ (near the surface).

whyOthersWrong.A: Acceleration is not a force.
whyOthersWrong.B: Mass is inertia; it is not the force of attraction.
whyOthersWrong.C: “Gravity” is vague; weight is the specific gravitational force on the body.
whyOthersWrong.D: Impulse is force × time, not static attraction.

whyOthersWrong.A: $1.5\times10^3$ is far too small and does not follow $c/n$.
whyOthersWrong.C: Wrong order of magnitude.
whyOthersWrong.D: This would exceed $c$ in vacuum for $n>1$, which is impossible.

whyCorrect: Relative density 19 means density $\approx 19\ \mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}$ relative to water. Mass $m=\rho V = 19\times 20 = 380\ \mathrm{g}$ (the metal’s mass; immersion does not change mass).

whyOthersWrong.B: 400 g would correspond to density 20 if volume is 20 cm³ — inconsistent with RD 19.
whyOthersWrong.C: 360 g does not match $\rho V$.
whyOthersWrong.D: 39 g is far too small.
whyOthersWrong.E: 180 g does not follow from RD 19 and 20 cm³.

whyCorrect: Hydrostatic pressure at a depth depends on fluid density and depth, not on the shape of the container.

whyOthersWrong.A: Pressure increasing with depth is correct.
whyOthersWrong.B: Same level same pressure in a static liquid is correct.
whyOthersWrong.C: Isotropic pressure at a point is correct.
whyOthersWrong.D: Force on wall perpendicular to wall is correct.

whyCorrect: Echo time is $2d/v$. Change in distance $\Delta d = v\,\Delta t/2 = 250\times(3.5-2.5)/2 = 125\ \mathrm{m}$.

whyOthersWrong.A: 10 m is too small for the given time difference and speed.
whyOthersWrong.C: 175 m does not match $v\Delta t/2$.
whyOthersWrong.D: 350 m corresponds to forgetting the factor of 2 for echo path.
whyOthersWrong.E: 1000 m is inconsistent with the data.

whyCorrect: Statement III is incorrect: beats arise from **interference** of two waves of slightly different frequencies, not because one wave travels faster than the other in the same medium. Past keys often pair III with another incorrect clause; verify IV/V wording against your source if unsure.

whyOthersWrong.A: I and II are individually correct statements.
whyOthersWrong.B: II is correct; III is wrong but II is not wrong.
whyOthersWrong.C: Duplicate in original options; I and II are both correct, not “NOT correct”.
whyOthersWrong.E: IV is a correct beat-frequency example for many interpretations.

whyCorrect: Long sight (hyperopia) blurs near objects; short sight (myopia) blurs distant objects. Short sight is corrected with **diverging** lenses, not converging — so III is incorrect.

whyOthersWrong.A: II is also correct, so not I only.
whyOthersWrong.B: I is also correct, so not II only.
whyOthersWrong.D: Includes III, which is false.

whyCorrect: The ratio of frequencies in a resonance tube is typically a simple fraction based on the harmonic series.

whyOthersWrong.A: This ratio does not correspond to the harmonic relationship in a resonance tube.
whyOthersWrong.B: This ratio does not correspond to the harmonic relationship in a resonance tube.
whyOthersWrong.D: This ratio does not correspond to the harmonic relationship in a resonance tube.
whyOthersWrong.E: This ratio does not correspond to the harmonic relationship in a resonance tube.

whyCorrect: In equilibrium, the sum of the horizontal and vertical components of the forces must be zero, making II and III correct.

whyOthersWrong.A: I is not correct as it does not represent the equilibrium condition.
whyOthersWrong.B: II is correct, but III is also correct, so this option is incomplete.
whyOthersWrong.C: III is correct, but II is also correct, so this option is incomplete.
whyOthersWrong.E: I is not correct, making this option incorrect.

whyCorrect: The force of static friction increases to match the applied force until it reaches its maximum value, beyond which the brick will slide.

whyOthersWrong.B: The direction of friction is opposite to the direction of the applied force, not to the right.
whyOthersWrong.C: Adding another brick does not decrease the friction on the first brick.
whyOthersWrong.D: Friction is zero only when the brick is sliding, not when pulled.
whyOthersWrong.E: Turning the brick does not inherently change the friction unless the normal force changes.

whyCorrect: Using the formula for pressure, P = h * ρ * g, we can find the height of the water column supported by the same pressure.

whyOthersWrong.A: 0 m is incorrect as pressure will support a height.
whyOthersWrong.B: 10 m is too low for the given density.
whyOthersWrong.C: 13 m is not the correct calculation.
whyOthersWrong.E: 18 m is too high for the given density.

whyCorrect: The speed of a wave in a stretched wire is given by the formula $v = f \lambda$. For the fundamental frequency, $\lambda = 2L$, thus $v = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$ ms-1.

whyOthersWrong.D: This speed is too high for the given frequency and length.
